coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of silver nanoparticles using folic acid as a reagent for colorimetric and fluorimetric detections of 6-mercaptopurine at nanomolar concentration
coredata.dc:creator: Rawat, Karuna A.
Singhal, Rakesh Kumar
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
coredata.dc:description: A simple and versatile strategy was developed for dual detection (colorimetric and fluorimetric) of 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) using folic acid (FA) fabricated silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) as a probe. The optical properties (surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band and fluorescence) of FA-Ag NPs were strongly influenced by the addition of 6-MP. The abrupt change in absorbance of FA-Ag NPs was observed with increasing concentration of 6-MP, resulting a slight blue-shift in the SPR band and a color change from yellow to colorless, which confirms the oxidation of Ag0 NPs to Ag+ ion. Advantage is taken of the fact that FA is displaced from the surface of the Ag NPs, which progressively enhanced the fluorescence intensity of FA with increasing concentration of 6-MP. The perfect linear variations of absorbance at 399nm and emission peak at 448nm with 6-MP concentration was observed in range of 20•1000 and 5•300nM by UV•vis and fluorescence spectrometric methods, enabling the use of probe as a dual probe for quantification of 6-MP. Moreover, the probe shows excellent anti-disturbance ability when exposed to a series of interfering other organic, inorganic and biomolecules and can be applied to the determination of 6-MP in real samples with the detection limits of 13.2 and 2.3nM by colorimetric and fluorimetric methods, which suggests that the present method has a great potential of application for assay of 6-MP in pharmaceutical and biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 6-mercaptopurine
FA-Ag NPs
DLS
Colorimetric and fluorimetric methods

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of FA functionalized Ag NPs
Dual detection of 6-MPby FA- Ag NPs
Dual sensing ability of FA-Ag NPs towards 6-MP
FA-Ag NPs as a dual sensor for assay of 6-MP
Determination of 6-MP in pharmaceutical sample and biofluids

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST), India under the DST- Inspire Fellowship for Ph.D. Programme. Authors thank Head, Chemical Engineering Department, S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat for providing DLS instrument facilities. Authors are grateful to DST (SR/FT/CS-54/2010), SVNIT (Dean (R&C)/1503/2013-2014) and DAE-BRNS (37(2)/14/07/2015/BRNS/10401), India for financial supports.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for colorimetric and fluorimetric sensing of 6-MP using FA-Ag NPs as a dual probe.
(a) DLS data of FA-Ag NPs. (b) TEM images FA-Ag NPs, (c) size distribution graph and (d) TEM image of FA-Ag NPs with 6-MP.

(a) UV•vis absorption and (b) fluorescence spectra of FA-Ag NPs in presence of different drugs (chloramphenicol, fluorouracil, flutamide, neomycin, kanamycin, vancomycin, amoxicillin and 6-mercaptopurine, 0.1α/4M). All the samples are prepared in water, and the emission peak was observed at 448nm when excited at excitation at 358nm. All the spectra were measured at room temperature. Insets: Photographic images of corresponding solutions at day light and under UV light at 365nm.

Effect of pH on the (a) UV•vis absorption with and (b) fluorescence spectra of FA-Ag NPs in the presence of 6-MP using Tris-HCl pH in the range of 2.0•12.0. The other conditions are the same which were shown in Fig. 2. Insets: Photographic images of corresponding solutions at day light.

(a) UV•vis absorption spectra of FA-Ag NPs with addition of various concentrations of 6-MP in the range of 00•1000nM. The other conditions are the same which were shown in Fig. 2. (b) Photographic image of corresponding solutions.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of FA-Ag NPs with addition of various concentrations of 6-MP in the range of 00•1000nM. The other conditions are the same which were shown in Fig. 2. (b) Photographic image of corresponding solutions under UV light at 365nm.

UV•vis absorption of FA-Ag NPs with the addition of mixture of different chemical species, including inorganic (Na+, K+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cl∧, I∧, Br∧, NO3 ∧, SO4 2∧, Cr2O7 2∧, 1.0mM), organic molecules (ATP, AMP, BSA, GSH, isatin, guanosine, guanine, thymine, urea, uric acid, glucose and uracil, 1.0mM) and drugs (chloramphenicol, fluorouracil, flutamide, neomycin, kanamycin, vancomycin and amoxicillin, 1.0mM) with and without addition of 6-MP. Insets: Photographic images of corresponding solutions at day light. The other conditions are the same which were shown in Fig. 2.
Overview of FA-Ag NPs-based UV•vis and fluorescence spectrometric methods with the reported methods for the detection of 6-MP.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of silver nanoparticles using folic acid as a reagent for colorimetric and fluorimetric detections of 6-mercaptopurine at nanomolar concentration

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat, 395007, Gujarat, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: S. V. National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Chemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Center, Trombay, Mumbai, 400085, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and versatile strategy was developed for dual detection (colorimetric and fluorimetric) of 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) using folic acid (FA) fabricated silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) as a probe. The optical properties (surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band and fluorescence) of FA-Ag NPs were strongly influenced by the addition of 6-MP. The abrupt change in absorbance of FA-Ag NPs was observed with increasing concentration of 6-MP, resulting a slight blue-shift in the SPR band and a color change from yellow to colorless, which confirms the oxidation of Ag0 NPs to Ag+ ion. Advantage is taken of the fact that FA is displaced from the surface of the Ag NPs, which progressively enhanced the fluorescence intensity of FA with increasing concentration of 6-MP. The perfect linear variations of absorbance at 399nm and emission peak at 448nm with 6-MP concentration was observed in range of 20•1000 and 5•300nM by UV•vis and fluorescence spectrometric methods, enabling the use of probe as a dual probe for quantification of 6-MP. Moreover, the probe shows excellent anti-disturbance ability when exposed to a series of interfering other organic, inorganic and biomolecules and can be applied to the determination of 6-MP in real samples with the detection limits of 13.2 and 2.3nM by colorimetric and fluorimetric methods, which suggests that the present method has a great potential of application for assay of 6-MP in pharmaceutical and biological samples.

ce:section.ce:para: With the features of organic framework on metal nanoparticles surfaces, research interest in fabricating metal NPs with various organic ligands and their integrations with various analytical techniques for assaying of wide variety chemical species have been growing unabated [1•5]. Recent years, metal NPs-based dual optical (colorimetric and fluorimetric) sensing assays have sparked significant excitement in analytical sciences due to high surface to volume ratio with the realization of supramolecular assemblies, allowing them to act as versatile and selective portable platforms for the implementation of different sensing schemes for assay of various analytes with more convincing results [6•8]. As a result, silver (Ag) and gold (Au) NPs have been brought to the forefront of UV•vis and fluorescence-based analytical research because of their tunable optical properties, facile synthesis and surface modification for the development of portable optical analytical tools.

For example, Zhao tm)s functionalized carbon dots on the surfaces of Au NPs for colorimetric and fluorimetric detection of thiocyanate in tap water and saliva samples [9]. Cheng and co-workers modified Au NPs with fluorescein isothiocyanate for assay of cyanide by colorimetric and fluorimetric methods [10]. Zuber tm)s team developed a portable sensing platform for the detection of gold nanoparticles based on the absorption and fluorescence mechanisms [11]. Tao et al. developed fluorescence turn-on and colorimetric dual channel probe for the detection of Hg2+ ion using poly(acrylic acid)-template Ag nanoclusters as a probe [12]. Mi tm)s group synthesized chitosan capped Ag NPs for fluorescence and colorimetric detections of Hg2+ ion in water samples [13]. Unmodified Ag NPs were used as sensors for dual functional colorimetric assays of both Hg2+ ion and H2O2 via Ag NPs catalyzed reaction [14]. Similarly, Su et al. developed FA-Ag NPs-based colorimetric and “turn-on” fluorescent sensor for the detection of Hg2+ ion in water samples [15]. Cui tm)s group modified Ag NPs with Rhodamine B for the fabrication of dual probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of fenamithion in food samples [16]. The colorimetric and phosphorimetric signaling strategies were developed for sensitive assay of organophosphorus pesticides using Mn-ZnS quantum dots-Au NPs through the inner filter effect and the activity of acetylcholinesterase [17]. Zheng and co-workers fabricated Au NPs with triazolylcoumarin for the colorimetric and fluorimetric detections of lysozyme via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism [18]. Furthermore, fluorescent carbon dots were functionalized on the surfaces of Au NPs for dual readouts of glutathione [19] and bromelain [20] in biological samples. These reports illustrate that the advancements in fabricating of metal NPs with organic network allowed to act them as a dual probe for both colorimetric and fluorimetric assays of target analytes with high selectivity and sensitivity. Furthermore, these organic ligands have only been recognized as the stabilizers in the synthesis of metal NPs where they can be acted as specific analyte recognition via large shifts in their optical properties (red/blue shift and fluorescence turn- off/on). Therefore, one-step synthesis of metal NPs with the specific recognition is an attractive but challenging target to pursue.

6-Mercaptopurine is a sulfur analogue of adenine that was used as an anticancer and an immunosuppressive agent [21]. It also acts as an anti-metabolic purine antineoplastic agent for the treatment of acute lymphoid leukemia [22,23]. It was observed that 6-MP and its metabolites inhibit the function of RNaseH in DNA•RNA heteroduplex molecules through incorporation of deoxythioguanosine into DNA [24]. It shows serious side effects due to its cytotoxicity nature towards various cells [25], which confirms that its activity was also different with the change of its concentration in plasma [26]. Therefore, it is important to develop a simple and sensing analytical tool for the accurate identification and quantification of 6-MP in pharmaceutical and biological samples.

In this connection, several analytical methods have been reported for the determination of 6-MP, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [27], multi-walled carbon nanotube (MWCN) and nanocomposite-based voltammetry [28,29], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [30,31], capillary electrophoresis [32], Raman spectroscopy [33], UV•vis [34] and fluorescence [35] spectroscopic techniques, respectively. These techniques suffer from poor sensitivity [34], entails time-consuming and tedious sample preparations (drug extraction and purification of analytical samples prior to its identification). In order to minimize sample preparations with improved sensitivity, few methods have been described the integration of nanomaterials with UV•vis and fluorescence spectroscopic techniques for analysis of 6-MP in pharmaceutical and biological samples. For example, Chen tm)s group synthesized bovine serum albumin capped Au NPs for the development of logical switch fluorescent sensor for the detection of 6-MP in human serum samples [36]. Shen et al. analyzed 6-MP in human urine samples by fluorescence spectrometry using Au NPs as a fluorescent probe [37]. Cuin and co-workers synthesized Ag(I) and Au(I) complexes with 6-MP and studied their biological activities [38]. The Ag NPs were decorated with β-cyclodextrin and used as a substrate for the detection of 6-MP by surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [39]. Recently, Yuan tm)s group synthesized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and used as probes for detection of 6-MP human serum samples via fluorescence quenching mechanism [40]. Although these methods are shown to be promising tools for 6-MP detection, they usually need either complicated procedures or multiple steps for the modifications of metal NPs with organic molecules as well as they require addition of specific reagents in order to interact with 6-MP. Although few nanomaterials were used for assay of 6-MP, no Ag NPs-based dual (colorimetric and fluorimetric) sensor was reported for the assay of 6-MP. Furthermore, there is still a gap of improvement within these reported works especially in the synthesis of Ag NPs with specific ligand for selective and sensitive analysis of 6-MP by UV•vis and fluorescence spectrometric techniques. Consequently, as mentioned above, it is very challenge to develop Ag NPs-based dual sensor (colorimetric and fluorimetric) sensor for selective and sensitive detection of 6-MP in biological samples.

Herein, we report one-step FA mediated synthesis of Ag NPs for the development of logic switch dual sensor for colorimetric and fluorimetric detection of 6-MP in pharmaceutical and biological samples. The addition of 6-MP into FA-Ag NPs causes oxidation of Ag0 NPs to Ag+ ion, resulting a blue-shift in the SPR peak and a color change from yellow to colorless. As expected, the emission peak at 448nm was progressively increased with increasing concentration of 6-MP, indicating that the displacement of FA from Ag NPs, which facilitate to develop colorimetric and ‘turn-on tm) fluorescence probe for the detection of 6-MP in biological samples with high selectivity and sensitivity. This work opens up new perspective for the development of miniaturized dual (colorimetric and fluorimetric) spectrometric methods for selective and sensitive detection of 6-MP without any complicated procedure or instruments.

All reagents used were of analytical reagent grade. Tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris buffer), and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, (St. Louis, MO). Folic acid and 6-mercaptopurine were purchased from SRL Pvt. Ltd., India. Milli-Q-pure water was used throughout the experiments. The 6-MP tablet (50mg 6-MP, Zydus Cadila Pvt. Ltd., India) was purchased from local medical shop, Surat, Gujarat, India. Urine samples were collected from three healthy volunteers in Applied Chemistry Department, S.V. National Institute of Technology, Surat, Gujarat, India and the serum samples were obtained from Iyer tm)s pathological laboratories, Surat, Gujarat, India. The obtained biofluids were diluted to 50 folds with distilled water before further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of FA functionalized Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The FA-mediated synthesis of Ag NPs was carried out by one-step reaction. Standard solutions of FA and AgNO3 were prepared using water as solvent. Briefly, 1.0mL of 2.0mM FA was added drop wise into 10.0mL of 1.0mM AgNO3 under vigorous stirring and then stirred for 60min at room temperature. The color of solution was changed from colorless to yellow, confirming the formation of FA-Ag NPs. The absorption spectra, size and morphology of FA-Ag NPs were characterized by UV•vis spectrometry, DLS and TEM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual detection of 6-MPby FA- Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The dual detection of 6-MP was carried out by the following procedure using FA-Ag NPs as a dual probe. In a typical procedure, different concentrations of 1.0mL 6-MP were added separately into 1.0mL of FA-Ag NPs solutions that were contained 30α/4L of Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.0). The above mixtures were equilibrated for 30min at room temperature. After that, UV•vis and fluorescence spectrometric techniques were used to measure the absorption and emission spectra of the above solutions at room temperature. The color changes of the above solutions were studied at day light and at dark under UV light illumination at 365nm.

UV•vis absorption and emission spectra of the solutions were measured by Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics, USA) and Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies) at room temperature. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on Tecnai 20 (Philips, Holland) at an acceleration voltage of 100kV. DLS data were obtained by Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a FTIR-8400S (Shimadzu, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy•energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (SEM•EDX) analysis was carried out using Hitachi Series 3400N, Japan, SEM operating at 30kV. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) data was obtained using Rigaku Mainflex, Japan.

As shown in Scheme 1 , a wide range of FA molecular networks was constructed on the surfaces of Ag NPs where FA was used as a reducing agent as well as molecular template. Such networks with well-defined geometries can selectively recognize trace target analytes with high sensitivity. Firstly, FA carries carboxylic acid and amino group those can capable for coordinating Ag+ ions with good geometry [15], which can involve in the reduction of Ag+ ion to Ag0 NPs and then construction of FA molecular networks on the surfaces of Ag NPs. In order to control the size and SPR peak of Ag NPs, we studied various parameters such as effect of FA concentration, effect of temperature and reaction time for effective molecular assembly of FA on the surfaces of Ag NPs.

Folic acid (conjugate base folate) is a B vitamin, and consist amino and carboxylic acid groups. We assume that amino group of FA plays key role to reduce Ag+ ion to Ag0 using AgNO3 as a precursor, which indicates that optimization of FA concentration is very important to control optical properties of Ag NPs. In this connection, the influence of FA concentration was investigated by keeping AgNO3 concentration at 1.0mM and varying the FA concentration over the range of 0.5•2.5mM. As shown in Supporting Information of Fig. S1, the FA shows characteristic absorption peak at 331nm. At the same time, the appearance of a new band at 399nm was occurred by the addition of FA into AgNO3 solutions, which is the characteristic SPR band of Ag NPs (Supporting Information of Fig. S2a). However, the SPR band at 399nm was gradually increased with increasing the concentration up to 2.0mM and then the absorption peaks of FA and Ag NPs were merged with peak broadening which might be explained due to the widening of the particle size distribution. Based on these observations, we found that the addition of 5.0mL of 2.0mM aqueous FA solution in to 1.0mM of aqueous AgNO3 (50mL) results in the optimum production of FA-Ag NPs as evidenced by the intensity, stability and sharpness of the surface plasmon resonance band.

The reduction rate Ag+ ion drastically depends on temperature, so we studied the effect of temperature on the absorption spectra of FA-Ag NPs. Supporting Information of Fig. S2b shows the UV•vis absorption spectra of FA-Ag NPs at different temperatures ranging from 0 to 100°C. It can be observed that the formation rate of FA-Ag NPs was very slow at low temperatures. It is important to notice that the color intensity of FA-Ag NPs was gradually increased with increasing temperature, however at 100°C, the color of FA-Ag NPs was completely changed to yellow, which confirms that the formed FA-Ag NPs were different shapes with larger diameters as well as formation of chain structures [41]. The UV•visible spectra of FA-Ag NPs reveal that the evolution of two spectral peaks at 408 and 428nm as a function of reaction temperature at 50 and 75°C. It can be noticed that the intensities of SPR peaks of FA-Ag NPs solutions at 50 and 75°C are broader than the FA-Ag NPs formed at 25°C, indicating that the synthesized FA-Ag NPs are not in uniform size. However, at 25°C, the maximum absorption band was observed at 399nm and the absorption bands at longer region were not appeared, which confirms that the complete synthesis of Ag NPs with an average size of 28±6.6nm and spherical shape. The size and morphology of synthesized FA-Ag NPs were corroborated by DLS, SEM, XRD and TEM. Therefore, we selected 25°C as an optimum reaction temperature for the synthesis of Ag NPs with an average size of 28±6.6nm using FA as a reducing and capping agent.

Furthermore, a time dependent UV•visible spectroscopic study was then carried out for the synthesis of FA-Ag NPs. As quickly as possible, UV•visible spectra of FA-Ag NPs were measured at every 15min for 75min and the obtained spectra were shown in Supporting Information of Fig. S3. It can be seen that the SPR peak intensity of FA-Ag NPs at 399nm was progressively increased with increasing reaction time from 0 to 60min and then there is no change in their intensity. For spherical Ag NPs, the SPR band maximum generally falls between 395 and 415nm [42]. Based on this assumption, we selected 60min as an optimum reaction time for the synthesis of FA-Ag NPs, which shows the ΢max value of 399nm that can be likely to apply to particles with diameter of <28nm. Taking the effect of FA concentration, temperature and reaction time into consideration, the optimum conditions were chosen to be 2.0mM of FA, 25°C temperature and 60min time for the synthesis of FA-Ag NPs with an average size of 28±6.6nm.

As expected, the formation of FA-Ag NPs was confirmed by the appearance of SPR band around 399nm as well as the formation of yellow color. Fig. S3 shows the absorption spectra of FA-Ag NPs, which displays a pronounced peak at 399nm, as expected from the SPR band of FA-Ag NPs. It can also be noticed that the emission peak of pure FA at 448nm was drastically quenched by the reduction of Ag+ ion to Ag0 NPs where FA acts as a reducing and stabilizing agent, yielding the quenching of blue color emission of pure FA under UV light illumination at 365nm, which confirms the formation of FA-Ag NPs. In order to see the functional group changes in FA after its oxidation, we studied the FT-IR spectra of pure FA and FA-Ag NPs (Supporting Information of Fig. S4). The hydroxyl (-OH) group of glutamic acid moiety of FA gives peak at 3321cm∧1 and the bands at 3541 and 3412cm∧1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of NH2 group of pteridine ring in FA. Moreover, the bands in the region of 1513•1485cm∧1 are ascribed to distinctive absorption band of phenyl and pteridine rings. The strong band around 1693cm∧1 belongs to stretching vibration of CO group while the band at 1606cm∧1 corresponds to the bending mode of NH group of FA [43,44]. The bands at 912 and 666cm∧1 are attributed to stretching vibration of CH bond. However, it can be observed that the spectral environment of FA was completely changed due its oxidation to reduce Ag+ ion to Ag0 NPs followed by its molecular assembly on the surfaces of Ag NPs. Since the characteristic bands of amino group at 3541 and 3412cm∧1 were almost disappeared in the spectrum of FA-Ag NPs and the characteristic bands of COOH, amide, phenyl and pterin rings were slightly shifted, which confirms that the formation of FA-Ag NPs. The size and morphology of FA-Ag NPs were characterized by of DLS, TEM, SEM and XRD techniques and the results were displayed in Fig. 1 and Supporting Information of Fig. S5. A mean hydrodynamic diameter of FA-Ag NPs was found to be 28±6.6nm (Fig. 1). TEM and SEM images and particle size distribution graph show that the as-synthesized FA-Ag NPs sizes are well dispersed and the particles sizes are found to be in between 20 and 25nm (Fig. 1), which is in good agreement with the DLS data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual sensing ability of FA-Ag NPs towards 6-MP
ce:section.ce:para: In order to develop FA-Ag NPs as a dual sensor, the absorbance and fluorescence changes of FA-Ag NPs were studied by the addition of various drugs (choloramphinecol, fluorouracil, flutamide, neoamycin, kanamycin, vancomycin, amoxicillin and 6-MP, 0.1mM) that are commonly present either in the compositions with 6-mercaptopurine or with similar mode of action. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the characteristic SPR band of FA-Ag NPs at 399nm was blue-shifted to 342nm, indicating that the oxidation of Ag0 NPs to Ag+ ion, which made the color of solution changes from yellow to colorless. As expected, the fluorescence emission peak at 448nm was drastically increased upon the addition of 6-MP when excited at 358nm, confirming that the interaction of 6-MP with Ag NPs, which causes the displacement of FA molecules on the surfaces of Ag NPs (Fig. 2b). As a result, FA-Ag NPs solution exhibits strong blue emission color only by the addition of 6-MP under UV light illumination at 365nm. The results demonstrate that the addition of known concentration of choloramphinecol, fluorouracil, flutamide, neoamycin, kanamycin, vancomycin and amoxicillin have no obvious effect on the SPR band, emission spectra and color of FA-Ag NPs as compared to 0.1mM of 6-MP, indicating that FA-Ag NPs act as a dual probe for selective and sensitive colorimetric and fluorimetric assays of 6-MP in pharmaceutical and biological samples.

In order to understand the interaction of FA-Ag NPs with 6-MP, we characterized FA-Ag NPs and FA-Ag NPs ∧6-MP by SEM, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) and X-ray diffraction spectroscopic techniques, respectively (Supporting Information of Figs. S5). It can be noticed that the monodispersed spherical Ag NPs were obtained using FA as a reducing and capping agent (Supporting Information of Fig. S5a). However, the monodispersed spherical Ag NPs were almost disappeared by the addition of 6-MP because of the deformation of Ag NPs via Ag-6-MP coordination complexes through S and N atoms of 6-MP to the silver surface (Supporting Information of Fig. S5b). EDX data provides qualitative as well as quantitative information of elements that may be involved in formation of Ag NPs. The elemental profile of Ag NPs using FA as a reducing and capping agent shows higher counts at 3keV due to the presence of silver, confirming that the formation of FA-Ag NPs (Supporting Information of Fig. S5c). Furthermore, Ag NPs exhibit typical optical absorption peak at 3keV due to their SPR property. The EDX spectrum of FA-Ag NPs confirmed the presence of elemental silver signals along with C and O elements. The elemental analysis of FA-Ag NPs revealed that the highest proportion of silver element. However, it can be clearly seen that the intensity of Ag was drastically decreased in the EDX of FA-Ag NPs-6-MP due to the deformation of Ag NPs (Supporting Information of Fig. S5d), which is well accordance with TEM and SEM data. Supporting Information of Fig. S5e shows the XRD pattern of FA-Ag NPs. The peak at about 38.45° was assigned to the (111) plane of faced-centered cubic (fcc) Ag crystal, indicating that FA-Ag NPs were composed of crystalline Ag.

Furthermore, pH of the NPs solution plays key role in sensing of target analytes. In this connection, we studied the absorption and fluorescence spectra of FA-Ag NPs (1.0mL) in presence of 6-MP (1.0mL, 0.5α/4M) using Tris-HCl buffer pH ranging from 2.0 to 12.0 (Fig. 3 ). It was noticed that the absorption spectrum of FA-Ag NPs with 6-MP showed maximum blue-shift with high intensity, indicating that the interaction of 6-MP with FA-Ag NPs is high at Tris-HCl pH 7.0. As a result, the emission peak intensity of FA-Ag NPs in the presence of 6-MP is very high at Tris-HCl pH 7.0, confirming that the displacement of FA molecules on the surfaces of Ag NPs, which results a strong blue color emission under UV light illumination at 365nm. Supporting Information of Fig. S6 shows the fluorescence spectra of FA-Ag NPs with addition of 6-MP in presence of Tris-HCl buffer at different pH from 2.0 to 12.0. Based on these observations, we selected Tris-HCl pH 7.0 as an optimum pH for dual assay of 6-MP.

In this work, we have selected folic acid as a reagent that can be used for the reduction as well as surface modification, since it contains COOH and NH2 groups, which can promote the reduction of Ag+ ions to Ag atoms followed by their molecular assembly on the surfaces of Ag NPs (Scheme 1). It was noticed that the FA (2.0mM) of solution was a slightly yellowish color and exhibited maximum absorbance at 331nm, as the redox (reduction of Ag+ ion and oxidation of FA) reaction was proceeded, the solution became dark yellow color, confirming that the fabrication of FA-Ag NPs, which shows the SPR band at 399nm. As expected, the fluorescence emission peak of FA at 448nm was completely quenched due to the oxidation of FA to reduce Ag+ ions, which indicates that it can be acted as a sensor for the development of fluorescence turn-off and turn-on mechanism. Therefore, only the addition of 6-MP caused a blue shift in the SPR band with broadening peak, suggesting that the strong interactions between 6-MP and FA-fabricated Ag NPs, which results the disappearance of the translucent yellow color of FA-fabricated Ag NPs. These changes indicate that the bleaching of SPR absorption band by the oxidation of Ag NPs in the presence of 6-MP, suggesting that the reorientation of FA molecules with respect to the silver surface or the strong adsorption of 6MP with Ag substrate, thereby favoring the deformation of Ag NPs and the formation of Ag-6-MP coordination complexes via S and N atoms of 6-MP to the silver surface [45], which is well accorded with TEM image of FA-Ag NPs-6-MP (Fig. 1d). As a result, oxidized FA molecules on the surfaces of Ag NPs can be replaced by 6-MP due to the strong affinity of 6-MP towards Ag NPs, since the thiol group (∧SH) of 6-MP was adsorbed more strongly than FA because of ∧SH donating a higher electron density into the Ag NPs [45•47]. As expected, the fluorescence turn-on could be realized by the addition of 6-MP, thereby the emission peak of FA at 448nm was greatly enhanced and blue emission was restored, which is due to the coordination effect between 6-MP and Ag surfaces. As a result, oxidized FA molecules can be replaced by 6-MP through the electron transfer, which results to restore the fluorescence of FA. Therefore, a fluorescence sensor could be developed for the detection of 6-MP based on the fluorescence turn-on (restoring).

ce:section.ce:section-title: FA-Ag NPs as a dual sensor for assay of 6-MP
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the sensitivity of the sensor for both UV∧visible and fluorescence detection of 6-MP, the absorption and emission spectra of FA-fabricated Ag NPs solution in different concentrations of 6-MP were measured. Under optimized conditions, different concentrations of 6-MP were added separately to the solutions of FA-fabricated Ag NPs and incubated for 2min. As shown in Fig. 4 a, upon the addition of increasing concentrations of 6-MP, the SPR band emerged as a blue shift with the disappearance of yellow color (Fig. 4b), which was attributed to the oxidation of Ag NPs to Ag+ ion and its strong coordination to 6-MP. Therefore, the absorbance peak of FA-fabricated Ag NPs was gradually decreased with increasing the concentration of 6-MP and there is a linear relationship was obtained between ΢A and the concentration of 6-MP over the range from 10 to 1000nM (y=0.003x+0.944, R2
=0.983) (Supporting Information of 7a). The results suggest that FA-fabricated Ag NPs act as a probe for the detection of 6-MP with a detection limit of 13.2nM calculated by a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (3σ/S). Where, S is the slope of the linear equation and σ is the standard deviation of blank.

Similarly, the emission peak intensity of FA at 448nm was progressively enhanced with increasing concentration of 6-MP in the range of 5•300nM, and even as low a concentration as 20 of 6-MP can give a distinct fluorescent enhancement (Fig. 5 a). A linear relationship between the fluorescent enhancement efficiency [(I•I0)/I0] and the concentration of 6-MP was observed in the range of 20•300nM, where the calibration equation was (I•I0)/I0 =0.773x ∧ 4.254íC6-MP (R2
=0.990) (Supporting Information of Fig. S7b). The detection limit of the fluorescence spectrum changes calculated on the basis of 3σ/m is 2.3nM for 6-MP, which is lower than that of the reported methods in the literature [26•29,35,36,40]. The detectable limit of 6-MP was 120nM at UV-lamp by the naked-eye (Fig. 5b). Table 1 shows the comparison of the present method with the reported methods for the detection of 6-MP.

To realize the selectivity of this probe toward 6-MP, the competitive chemical species, including inorganic ions (Na+, K+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cl∧, I∧, Br∧, NO3 ∧, SO4 2∧, Cr2O7 2∧, 1.0mM), organic molecules (ATP, AMP, BSA, GSH, isatin, guanosine, guanine, thymine, urea, uric acid, glucose and uracil, 1.0mM) and drugs (chloramphenicol, fluorouracil, flutamide, neomycin, kanamycin, vancomycin and amoxicillin, 1.0mM) were examined under optimized conditions (Fig. 6 and Supporting Information of Fig. S8). As expected, the SPR band at 399nm was blue-shifted and the emission peak of FA at 448nm was enhanced even in the presence of other chemical species, whereas the other chemical species did not induce either spectral or color changes. These results revealed that the absorption and emission spectra of FA-Ag NPs with 6-MP were not significantly influenced by the subsequent addition of competing other chemical species (inorganic ions, biomolecules and drugs). Similarly, the FA-Ag NPs switching behavior (fluorescence “turn-on”) was not affected even in the presence of inorganic species, drugs and biomolecules together. Therefore, FA-Ag NPs shows marvelous selectivity towards 6-MP, revealing that FA-Ag NPs act as a potential probe for assay of 6-MP in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of 6-MP in pharmaceutical sample and biofluids
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the potential application of this sensing system in real samples, the sensor was successfully applied to detect 6-MP in pharmaceutical (Tablet 6-MP, 6-MP ∧ 50mg, Zydus Cadila Pvt. Ltd., India), and biological (urine and serum) samples, respectively. The procured tablet 6-MP was crushed and dissolved in 50mL of deionized water, and then sonicated for 20min. Then, the samples were diluted to appropriate volume in order to obtain working standard solution and analyzed by the aforesaid procedure. For biological samples (urine and serum), the serum and urine samples were collected from three healthy volunteers and then centrifuged at 3000rpm for 10min. After thawing, the collected samples were diluted 50 times with distilled water and then spiked with different concentration of 6-MP. The concentration of 6-MP was estimated by the aforesaid procedure. The amount of 6-MP was successfully estimated as 49.5mg in tablet 6-MP, which is well accorded with the specification of 50mg. Comfortingly, satisfactory recoveries of 6-MP were obtained in urine and plasma samples, with recoveries of over 96.7•97.7%, as shown in Table S1. Importantly, the results presented in Table S1 also demonstrated that the compositions of real/pharmaceutical samples did not significantly interfere with the assay of 6-MP, indicating the potential applications of the present method for the assay of 6-MP in pharmaceutical and biological samples with minimized sample preparations.

The accuracy and precision of the method was studied as intra- and inter- day variations. Intra- and inter- day accuracy and precision were determined by analyzing 500, 750 and 1000nM of 6-MP in tablet 6-MP, urine and plasma samples for three times in the same day and daily for 3days over the period of week. Intra- and inter- day UV•vis absorption spectra of FA-Ag NPs with 6-MP were shown in Supporting Information of Fig. S9. The accuracy and precision of the method were found to be ∧2.9•1.0 and 0.1•1.2 (Supporting Information of Table S1), which indicate that the present method exhibits good accuracy and precision for the assay of 6-MP in pharmaceutical and biological samples.

In summary, FA-fabricated Ag NPs was demonstrated to be a high performance colorimetric and fluorescence “turn-on” probe for the detection of 6-MP in pharmaceutical and biological samples. It shows high selectivity, good sensitivity, and fast response toward 6-MP and other chemical species did not disturb with detection of 6-MP by both UV•vis and fluorescence spectroscopic methods. In comparison with previous reports, this assay presents the following unique features such as 6-MP was simply and sensitively detected at as low as 4.8 (colorimetric) and 2.3nM (fluorimetric) by the spectral changes and at 25nM (day light) and 120nM (UV light) by the naked eye without any tedious sample preparation. In addition, this probe was successfully applied to detect 6-MP in tablet 6-MP and biological samples with good accuracy and precision. Therefore, this assay provided a fast and simple method for the assay of 6-MP in real samples with high selectivity and sensitivity, and will find potential applications in assaying of 6-MP in pharmaceutical and clinical samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST), India under the DST- Inspire Fellowship for Ph.D. Programme. Authors thank Head, Chemical Engineering Department, S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat for providing DLS instrument facilities. Authors are grateful to DST (SR/FT/CS-54/2010), SVNIT (Dean (R&C)/1503/2013-2014) and DAE-BRNS (37(2)/14/07/2015/BRNS/10401), India for financial supports.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.04.018.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Karuna A. Rawat is pursuing his Ph.D under the supervision of Dr. Suresh Kumar Kailasa at the Department of Applied Chemistry, S.V. National Institute of Technology, Surat, India. She is interested in metal nanoparticles as sensors for biomolecules.

Rakesh Kumar Singhal passed BARC Training School (32nd Batch) from Chemistry stream. He has obtained his master degree in chemistry from I.I.T. Delhi and Ph.D. (Chemistry) from Mumbai University in 1996. He is the Head, Analytical Spectroscopy Section of Analytical Chemistry Division of Bhabha Atomic Research Center, India is responsible for development of analytical spectroscopic techniques for the measurement of traces of various elements in different environmental matrices and samples originating from different chemical processes. He has authored more than 150 publications in International & national Journals, Symposium and Book Chapters, in the field of environmental & radioanalytical chemistry.

Suresh Kumar Kailasa completed his Ph. D. in Chemistry from S. V. University, Tirupati in 2005. After completing his two postdoctoral fellowships at South Korea and at National Sun Yat-Sen University, Taiwan he joined as a faculty at S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat in 2009. He received Young Scientist Award from Taiwan Mass Spectrometry Society in 2013. His interest includes nanomaterials integration in MALDI-MS and development of nanomaterials-based colorimetric sensors for biomolecules, drugs and metal ions.
